---
title: heormodel
author: Pedro Nascimento de Lima
---

Health economic evaluation in Python: parameter sampling, simulation across model types, cost-effectiveness analysis, and value-of-information analysis for model-based health technology assessment.

## Install

```bash
pip install heormodel
```

The `calibration` extra adds Bayesian calibration: `pip install "heormodel[calibration]"`.

## Quickstart

A probabilistic sensitivity analysis table from any source enters the analysis through one call, `as_outcomes`. From the resulting object, run incremental cost-effectiveness analysis and value of information:

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from heormodel.run import as_outcomes
from heormodel.cea import icer_table
from heormodel.voi import evpi

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 2_000
df = pd.concat(
    pd.DataFrame({
        "strategy": name, "iteration": range(n),
        "cost": rng.normal(cost, 2_000, n), "qaly": rng.normal(q, 0.4, n),
    })
    for name, cost, q in [("Standard care", 40_000, 8.0), ("New drug", 52_000, 8.6)]
)

outcomes = as_outcomes(df)
icer_table(outcomes).round(1)
```

The `icer_table` orders strategies by cost and reports incremental cost-effectiveness ratios along the efficiency frontier; the `status` column flags dominated (`D`) and extended-dominated (`ED`) strategies. The expected value of perfect information reads off the same object:

```{python}
print(f"EVPI at WTP 30,000: {evpi(outcomes, wtp=30_000):,.1f}")
```

## How it fits together

One parameter object flows through swappable model engines into a shared analysis layer. Every engine returns costs and effects in one standardized structure, `Outcomes`, indexed by `(strategy, iteration)`, so `icer_table`, `ceac`, and `evpi` behave the same whether the numbers come from a spreadsheet export, a decision tree function, or a future engine. You do not need an engine to start: bring your own table, as the quickstart does.

[Engines](concepts/engines.qmd) covers the contract every engine satisfies and the shared iteration index that the value-of-information estimators rely on.

## Where to go next

- [Get started](get-started.qmd): install and run your first analysis.
- [Bring your own outputs](tutorials/byo-outputs.qmd): an external results table through cost-effectiveness analysis, value of information, plots, and a run report.
- [Markov cohort model](tutorials/mdm-cohort.qmd): the standard state-transition model, then the [microsimulation engine](tutorials/microsim.qmd) as the step up in detail.
- [Full pipeline](tutorials/full-pipeline.qmd): parameters, `run_psa`, cost-effectiveness analysis, and value of information end to end.
- [Calibration workflow](tutorials/calibration-workflow.qmd): calibrate some parameters, take the rest from the literature, mix both with `mix_draws`, then run the same analyses.
- [API reference](reference/index.qmd): every public function, generated from docstrings.
- [Changelog](changelog.md): what has shipped.
